nativeRange.habitat: Lagoon and seaward reefs, often on jetty pillars, sponges, and other sheltered hard structures; benthic at depths from the shallows down to around 70 m, though typically encountered above 20 m

diet.notes: Ambush predator that lures prey with a modified first dorsal spine (illicium) tipped with a fleshy esca shaped like a small fish or shrimp. Generally feeds on fish and crustaceans, and has been reported to engulf prey nearly equal to its own body length. New specimens typically only accept live feeder shrimp or small marine fish at first; many can be trained over time to take frozen krill, silversides, or pieces of marine fish from tongs. Feeding every 3–5 days is generally advised, as overfeeding has been linked to bloat and food refusal.

goodWith: large peaceful fish too big to be swallowed

summary: A large benthic frogfish that lures prey with a modified dorsal-fin appendage. Best suited to intermediate keepers in a species-focused tank; will swallow tankmates close to its own body length.
careNotes:
The Black Angler (Antennarius commerson) is the dark color phase of Commerson's Frogfish, one of the largest Antennariidae and a famously cryptic ambush predator. Body coloration is highly variable across the species — cream, pink, yellow, orange, red, green, brown, and black phases are all reported — and individuals can shift hue over a period of days to match their surroundings. The 'Black Angler' trade name refers specifically to dark-pigmented specimens; husbandry needs are identical across color morphs.

The fish hunts by wiggling a modified first dorsal spine (illicium) tipped with a fleshy lure (esca) that mimics a small fish or shrimp, then lunging forward with an extremely fast strike to vacuum prey into its mouth. It has been documented swallowing prey roughly equal to its own length, so any small fish or motile invertebrate in the same tank is generally at risk.

Sources disagreed substantially on minimum tank size: LiveAquaria suggests 30 gallons (~114 L), Saltwaterfish.com 50 gallons (~190 L), ReefApp 200 L, and several frogfish-husbandry guides (reefs.com, hobby threads) recommend 100–120 gallons (~380–450 L) for an adult given its eventual ~38–45 cm body length and predatory feeding style. Following the AquaMate tank-size convention of biasing toward the larger value for predators that 'eat fish their own size', a 300-L minimum was chosen, rounded to a common aquarium volume; aquarists raising juveniles can start smaller, but should plan for an adult footprint.

Reported maximum size also varied: LiveAquaria and Wikipedia state ~38 cm, while FishBase and ReefApp state 45 cm. An average maximum of ~42 cm was used.

Generally considered reef-safe with respect to corals, though contact with stinging anemones (especially elegance anemones) and certain LPS has been reported to injure the scaleless skin; the fish is decidedly NOT safe with small fish, shrimp, or ornamental crustaceans. Strong conspecific aggression is well documented — wild individuals are solitary and females have been reported to attack or even consume males outside of brief spawning encounters.

Feeding is the principal husbandry hurdle: new specimens commonly refuse anything but live prey, and starvation is a real risk if a transition to frozen foods isn't established. Many keepers train the fish to take frozen silversides or krill from tongs by mimicking prey movement.

No venomous spines are documented for this species (unlike the related Striated Frogfish), and FishBase classifies it as harmless to humans.
breedingNotes: Oviparous. Wild spawnings produce a ribbon-like or veil-like gelatinous egg raft that drifts in the water column until hatching. Captive breeding has been recorded but is generally considered uncommon, and pair maintenance is difficult given that females have been reported to attack or eat males outside the brief spawning window.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Antennariidae, Lophiiformes), maximum length (45 cm TL), native distribution (Indo-Pacific and Eastern Pacific), depth range (0–70 m, usually <20 m), preferred temperature (24.5–29 °C, mean 27.9 °C), reproduction (oviparous, gelatinous egg raft), and harmless-to-humans rating.

additional.name: Wikipedia (Commerson's frogfish)
additional.notes: Taxonomy and biology cross-check. Confirmed maximum length 38 cm, solitary behavior, lure morphology, broad color polymorphism, and reports of female aggression toward males after spawning.

additional.name: ReefApp Encyclopedia
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (45 cm), tank size (200 L minimum), salinity (SG ~1.026), and reef-safety caveats (avoid stinging corals/anemones).

additional.name: Saltwaterfish.com (Black Angler product)
additional.notes: Used to triangulate tank-size recommendation (50 gal / ~190 L) and water parameters (75–82 °F, pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.020–1.025); replaces Reef2Reef wiki which returned 403.

fish.breedingNotes: Oviparous. Captive breeding is uncommon; eggs are released in a gelatinous ribbon or veil that drifts in the water column. Pair maintenance is difficult given documented female aggression toward males.
